steps.narrationText: First, set a secret in env mode. The value lands as an environment variable inside the container. First writes need no ceremony — it is overwriting an existing secret that requires proof.

Now a file mode secret. File mode never puts the value in the container environment. It writes it to a file at slash run slash secrets, using Docker standard mechanism. Prefer this for anything sensitive.
Let us list what we have. Names and modes only. The list never shows values, no matter who is asking.
Now the part that surprises people. The secret get command returns a digest, not the value. There is no flag that gives the plaintext back. That is the GitHub Actions model: write only by design.
Lost the value? Do not peek. Rotate. The rotate-secret flag skips the precondition check, and the audit log marks the change as a deliberate rotation. Loud, intentional, safe.
Time to prove the secrets actually work. Deploy the repo. The compose file consumes both: the env value through interpolation, the file value through a Docker secrets mount.
Did the env secret arrive? Ask the container to print the variable. There is the value: postgres dot internal. The app really got it.
And the file secret? Read it from slash run slash secrets inside the container. There it is, the rotated value. The app reads the plaintext; it is only the tooling that refuses to show it to you.
Now the punchline. We will fork the repo. The fork is a byte-for-byte copy of the parent encrypted image.
But when we list the fork secrets: empty. No Stripe key, no database password, no API token. The fork cannot pretend to be you. That is why clone production, break nothing actually works.

One more proof. Try to start the fork with the parent compose. It fails: the bind source path for the Stripe key does not exist under the fork. Production credentials never follow a fork, not even by accident.

narrationAudio.narrationText: Time to put real credentials in a place forks can't reach! We'll set two secrets, deploy the app and prove it really receives them, then fork the repo and watch the fork come up empty handed. It can't even start.

Most teams drop a dot env file into the repo because it's the obvious move. The problem is the fork inherits a byte-for-byte copy of the image, so your sandbox boots with the production Stripe key and starts charging real customer cards.
The CLI follows the GitHub Actions model: get returns a digest, never the value. If you lose track of a secret, you don't peek, you rotate, and the audit log records it as a deliberate change.

Here's the payoff. The fork shares the parent's encrypted data, but the secret store is scoped per repo, so the fork lists empty, can't interpolate any of your production keys, and can't even start the parent's compose.
And that's secrets locked down where forks can't follow. See you in the next one!
